phenomenon: Information asymmetry enabling deception despite institutional safeguards
claim: Legal restrictions on service quality prevent fraud more effectively than transparency mechanisms even though both should theoretically work equally well.

phenomenon: Job design clustering despite continuous strategy space
claim: Employers segregate into two distinct strategies (high-control or high-trust) only when they can observe worker reputation signals and competition forces selection on past performance.

phenomenon: Persistent bubble formation despite transparent fundamentals
claim: Subject misunderstanding of declining value process rather than excess liquidity drives market bubbles; context reframing reduces confusion and mispricing without reducing liquidity.

phenomenon: Incentive design that transforms public good into club good
claim: Giving donors priority for receiving organs increases registration when allocation system provides timely feedback about other donors' decisions, not merely from cost reduction.
Moderate punishment reduces welfare in noisy environments because mistaken punishments of cooperators reduce subsequent cooperation and create defection spirals.
Agents sustain efficient allocation through decentralized gift-exchange if discount factor exceeds threshold that depends on group size, with reciprocal behavior enforced by autarky threat.
